title: The model comparison

spec.title: The model comparison
spec.notes_for_generator: Same prompt, two chat-based LLMs or two clean sessions of the same LLM. Compare and evaluate outputs against explicit criteria. Builds model fluency and critical evaluation, not brand loyalty.

content:
SUBJECT: AI Gym Day 13: Same prompt, two models, one winner

Most people pick an AI tool and stick with it. Not because it's better — because it's familiar. Today you're going to find out what you're actually missing.

TODAY'S WORKOUT
───────────────
✍️ CREATION | 🔵 STEADY STATE | ⏱ 15–20 min

THE BRIEF:
You need a short promotional paragraph — around 100 words — announcing a new feature, service, or initiative to your audience. It could be a product update email, a LinkedIn post intro, or an internal newsletter item. Pick something real enough to feel like actual work. You're going to run the same prompt in two different chat-based LLMs (or two clean, separate sessions of the same one), then compare what comes back.

YOUR REPS:
1. Write a prompt that specifies: what you're announcing, who the audience is, what tone you want, and what the reader should feel or do after reading it. Keep the prompt identical across both sessions. Run it in LLM A. Then run the exact same prompt in LLM B (or a fresh session). Don't tweak anything between runs.

2. Read both outputs against these four criteria: Does it open with something that earns attention? Is the tone consistent throughout? Does it make the announcement clearly without over-explaining? Does it end with a logical next step or reaction? Score each output against each criterion — a simple pass/fail is fine. Note which fell short and where.

3. Take the stronger output and send a follow-up prompt to fix the one or two things it still got wrong. Then do the same for the weaker output. See if it closes the gap. The goal isn't a perfect paragraph — it's understanding what a targeted follow-up can and can't rescue.

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
Your output is good if:
• The final paragraph could be dropped into its intended format (email, post, newsletter) without structural changes
• You can name at least one specific difference in how the two models interpreted your prompt — not just "one was longer" but what choice each made about tone, structure, or emphasis
• Your follow-up prompts targeted something concrete from your evaluation, not a vague "make it better"

💡 COACHING NOTE:
Same input, different outputs — that gap is information. Models don't fail randomly; they make different interpretive choices about your prompt's ambiguity. When two models diverge, it usually means your prompt left something underspecified — and one model guessed differently than the other. The skill here isn't picking a favourite tool; it's learning to read outputs diagnostically. What did each model assume you meant? Once you can see that, you can close the ambiguity at the prompt level, which makes every future draft more predictable — regardless of which tool you're using.

──────────
You're two days from the end of the free block. The 90-Day AI Habit picks up where this leaves off — building the kind of working practice that actually sticks. You can join solo, bring a small group, or look into getting your employer to cover it. Details here: https://harrysharman.com/projects/ai-habit/

──────────
Use only material you are allowed to share with your chosen LLM. Anonymise anything sensitive.
Share the programme: https://harrysharman.com/projects/ai-habit/
Found this useful? You can leave a tip: https://ko-fi.com/theaihabit
review_notes: Clarified that AI Habit focuses on chat-based large language models (LLMs), while using AI as public shorthand.
Kept exercises model-agnostic and usable with any text-based LLM or approved work tool.
Reworked research/reasoning language to avoid over-trusting model confidence or hidden chain-of-thought.
Reframed AI Habit as a grounded free 14-day starter block with a paid continuation for deeper 60-90 day habit-building, rather than a validation experiment.

Positioned the paid continuation as The 90-Day AI Habit: a structured applied-LLM programme covering writing/editing, strategy/decision-making, research/assessment, management/delegation, personal productivity, and practical outputs.
Simplified the 90-day commercial route: join individually, bring a small group for a discounted group subscription, or use the workplace launch pack to get an employer to pay.
Added ethical upgrade nudges to Days 3, 7, and 10: endowed progress, career identity, and momentum protection for The 90-Day AI Habit.
Mechanical repair pass: normalised workout badge line after scheduler verification.
Regenerated on 2026-06-05 because original scheduled source was missing core workout sections.
